summary: DHH reflects on Baudrillard's concept of simulacra — the idea that fictional constructs like Disneyland exist to make us believe everything else is real — and how this insight resurfaced during the Canadian trucker protest and government response to frozen bank accounts. He connects Baudrillard's thinking to Harari's and Graeber's arguments that human institutions are fundamentally make-believe. The Canadian government's actions felt like a 'tear in reality' that made traditional banking seem like the fictional construct, leading him to reconsider cryptocurrency. He acknowledges post-modern philosophy can be 'obnoxiously pompous' but argues small doses can produce genuine insight.
key_points: Baudrillard's insight that Disneyland exists to make the rest of reality seem real applies broadly to human institutions
The Canadian trucker protest bank freezes made traditional financial systems suddenly feel like the fictional construct rather than the stable reality
Existential insights about the make-believe nature of institutions are hard to hold in constant consciousness — they dissolve into daily life until events force them back
Post-modern philosophy contains genuine insights but requires careful dosing to avoid getting lost in nonsense
This realization drove DHH's conversion to seeing value in Bitcoin and crypto as alternatives to traditional finance
money_quotes: Human institutions are all, broadly speaking, make believe.
It's the kind of insight that's almost too much to bear on a constant conscious basis.
Disneyland suddenly appears more real than the rest. Our contract with what we thought it meant to live in reality is reneged upon.
Like a tear in reality. Like the upside down version of maple leaf.
The difference between poison and elixir is indeed often just the dose. And a drop of Baudrillard can really stir the mental waters.
When the traditional money and the bank accounts suddenly seemed like the carton cut-out version of reality.
key_insight: When governments freeze bank accounts over political protest, the supposedly solid institutions of traditional finance start to look more like make-believe than the crypto alternatives.
